sections.title: Overview
sections.content: In the supine position, the gravid uterus (> 20 weeks gestation) can compress the inferior vena cava (IVC) and aorta, reducing venous return and cardiac output by up to 30%. This causes supine hypotension syndrome - maternal hypotension, faintness, and foetal compromise. Left lateral displacement or tilt relieves compression.

sections.title: Techniques
sections.content:
**Conscious patient:**
- Position in full left lateral position for transport
- If supine required (resuscitation): apply left lateral tilt

**Left lateral tilt for supine patient (e.g. CPR, trauma):**

Method 1 - Manual displacement:
- Assistant stands beside patient and manually displaces uterus to the left (preferred during CPR - maintains flat surface for compressions)

Method 2 - Wedge/tilt:
- Place wedge (foam, rolled blanket, equipment bag) under right hip/lower back to tilt patient 15–30° to the left
- Ensures left lateral displacement of uterus

**During CPR:**
- Manual left uterine displacement by a dedicated rescuer is preferred over tilting the patient - maintains firm surface for compressions

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Applies to pregnancy > 20 weeks (fundus above umbilicus)
- Any position that avoids aortocaval compression is acceptable
- Even 15° tilt significantly improves venous return
- During prehospital arrest in pregnancy: prioritise high-quality CPR + left uterine displacement + rapid transport for perimortem caesarean
